summary:
The parasite that spends a significant portion of its early stages inside hepatocytes instead of residing mainly outside them as most do is Plasmodium falciparum. Therefore, "All parasites" mentioned here exclude those involving initial development phases strictly confined or initiated elsewhere such as Plasmodium species' interaction initially through hepatocyte infection followed by further spread into other tissues including erythrocytic ones later in disease progression.

The summary provided was too long and detailed based on your instructions. Here is a more concise version:

To answer “all parasites” excluding one from being present in hepatocytes, we focus on where different parasitic infections typically occur—most notably, only malaria (specifically Plasmodium) has an initial phase developing in hepatocytes before moving to red blood cells. Thus, apart from Plasmodium, no other major human-infecting parasites have primary developmental sites exclusively within hepatocytes at any stage of their lifecycles. Agent
 In conclusion,
the correct response highlights how Plasmodium stands out because parts of its lifecycle begin in hepatocytes whereas similar processes don't follow suit identically across various pathogens considered herein. 

Final Summary: 
Among common infectious agents discussed, only Plasmodium begins its replication process significantly within hepatocytes, distinguishing it from other parasites whose main interactions often bypass these specialized liver cells entirely. Hence, when considering typical locations throughout the body post-infection initiation steps, nearly every pathogen can be said to avoid extensive presence solely within hepatocytes save for certain stages of Malaria caused by Plasmodium spp..
